title: Windows Process
summary: A running instance of a program with its own virtual address space, handles and threads.
purpose: Processes are the unit of execution and isolation in Windows. Each has a PID, a parent (PPID), a token defining its privileges, and an integrity level.
architecture: A process owns a private virtual address space, one or more threads, loaded modules (DLLs), and handles to kernel objects. Creation flows through CreateProcess -> kernel process/thread objects.
dependencies: ntdll.dll and the loader map dependencies; the token comes from the parent or an explicit logon.
windows_logs: Security 4688 (process creation, with command line if auditing is enabled) and 4689 (process exit).
linux_logs: auditd execve records; /var/log/auth.log for privilege changes.
sysmon: Event ID 1: Process Create (hashes, full command line, parent image)
detection: Baseline parent-child relationships, alert when Office apps spawn shells, and verify image paths and signatures against expected locations.
sigma:
title: Office Spawning PowerShell
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  sel:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\WINWORD.EXE'
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
  condition: sel
hardening: Enable command-line auditing and Sysmon; apply Attack Surface Reduction rules to block Office child processes.
enterprise: Process telemetry is the backbone of EDR detections and endpoint threat hunting.

title: Windows Service
summary: A background program managed by the Service Control Manager, often running as SYSTEM.
purpose: Services provide always-on functionality (networking, printing, security) and start automatically without a user logged on.
architecture: The SCM (services.exe) reads service config from the registry (Start, ImagePath, ObjectName) and hosts many services inside shared svchost.exe groups.
dependencies: Most services depend on RpcSs/DcomLaunch; config lives under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services.
windows_logs: System 7045 (new service installed), 7036 (state change); Security 4697 (service installed).
sysmon: Event ID 1 for the hosting process
Event ID 13 for service registry changes
detection: Alert on 7045 with binaries outside System32, services running from user-writable paths, and svchost with an unexpected parent.
sigma:
title: Suspicious New Service Binary
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: system
detection:
  sel:
    EventID: 7045
    ImagePath|contains: '\Users\'
  condition: sel
hardening: Restrict service creation rights, fix unquoted paths, and monitor the Services registry hive.
enterprise: Autoruns and service baselining are core to persistence hunting.

title: Kernel Driver
summary: Kernel-mode code (.sys) running at Ring 0 to talk to hardware or extend the OS.
purpose: Drivers bridge hardware and the OS and can filter file, registry and network operations (used by both EDR and malware).
architecture: Drivers load per their Start type (Boot/System/Auto/Demand) and register with the I/O manager; Driver Signature Enforcement validates signatures.
dependencies: Most modern drivers build on the Kernel-Mode Driver Framework (Wdf01000.sys).
windows_logs: System 7026 (boot driver load failure); Code Integrity 3001/3033 (unsigned/blocked driver).
linux_logs: dmesg / kernel ring buffer; modprobe and lsmod for loaded modules.
sysmon: Event ID 6: Driver Loaded (image, signature status)
detection: Compare loaded drivers against the Microsoft vulnerable-driver blocklist; alert on unsigned or newly loaded kernel modules.
sigma:
title: Vulnerable Driver Loaded
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: sysmon
detection:
  sel:
    EventID: 6
    Signature|contains: 'unknown'
  condition: sel
hardening: Enable HVCI/Memory Integrity, the Microsoft vulnerable-driver blocklist, and kernel lockdown.
enterprise: BYOVD is a leading path to kernel compromise; driver telemetry is essential.

title: Windows Registry
summary: A hierarchical database of keys and values storing OS and application configuration.
purpose: The registry holds boot, service, security and per-user settings - and, as a result, most persistence real estate.
architecture: Organised into hives (HKLM, HKCU, ...) backed by files on disk. Keys contain typed values (REG_SZ, REG_DWORD, etc.).
dependencies: The SYSTEM hive is read during boot before most drivers and services.
windows_logs: Security 4657 (registry value modified) when object auditing is enabled.
detection: Monitor autostart locations and Winlogon keys with Sysmon 12/13; alert on Run values pointing to AppData or temp.
sigma:
title: Run Key Persistence
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: sysmon
detection:
  sel:
    EventID: 13
    TargetObject|contains: '\CurrentVersion\Run'
  condition: sel
hardening: Restrict write access to autostart keys and forward Sysmon registry events to the SIEM.
enterprise: Autoruns triage and registry baselining are standard IR steps.

title: LSASS & Credentials
summary: The Local Security Authority Subsystem enforces security policy and caches credential material.
purpose: lsass.exe validates logons and holds secrets (NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets) in memory - the prime credential-theft target.
architecture: Runs as SYSTEM from wininit.exe; authentication packages (msv1_0, kerberos) plug into it.
dependencies: Backed by the SAM/SECURITY hives and, in a domain, the domain controller.
sysmon: Event ID 10: ProcessAccess to lsass.exe (watch GrantedAccess 0x1010/0x1410)
detection: Alert on any non-system process opening lsass with VM_READ; watch for lsass.dmp file creation.
sigma:
title: LSASS Memory Access
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: sysmon
detection:
  sel:
    EventID: 10
    TargetImage|endswith: '\lsass.exe'
    GrantedAccess: '0x1410'
  condition: sel
yara: rule Mimikatz_Strings { strings: $a = "sekurlsa" ascii wide condition: $a }
hardening: Enable Credential Guard, RunAsPPL (LSASS protection), and restrict debug privilege.
enterprise: LSASS protection and detection are among the highest-value defensive controls.

title: PowerShell
summary: A powerful scripting and automation shell, and a favourite living-off-the-land tool for attackers.
purpose: PowerShell administers Windows at scale; its .NET access and in-memory execution make it ideal for fileless attacks.
architecture: The engine hosts scriptblocks; logging is provided by module, scriptblock and transcription logging.
dependencies: Relies on .NET; can call Win32 APIs directly.
windows_logs: PowerShell/Operational 4103 (module) and 4104 (scriptblock); Security 4688 for process start.
linux_logs: PowerShell Core logs to syslog when configured.
sysmon: Event ID 1 with the decoded command line
Event ID 3 for outbound download cradles
detection: Enable scriptblock logging (4104) and alert on -enc, hidden windows, DownloadString and AMSI bypass strings.
sigma:
title: Encoded PowerShell
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  sel:
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: ' -enc '
  condition: sel
hardening: Enable Constrained Language Mode, scriptblock logging, AMSI, and application control (WDAC).
enterprise: Scriptblock logging is one of the highest-signal Windows telemetry sources.

title: Kerberos
summary: The default domain authentication protocol, based on tickets issued by a Key Distribution Center.
purpose: Kerberos provides single sign-on using time-limited, encrypted tickets instead of transmitting passwords.
architecture: The client gets a TGT (AS exchange), then service tickets (TGS exchange), then presents them to services (AP exchange).
dependencies: Requires synchronised clocks (within 5 minutes) and DNS; the krbtgt account key encrypts all TGTs.
linux_logs: krb5 logs; SSSD logs on domain-joined Linux.
sysmon: Correlate 4769 bursts with the requesting process
detection: Alert on many 4769 RC4 (0x17) tickets from one account and on TGTs with anomalous lifetimes.
sigma:
title: Potential Kerberoasting
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  sel:
    EventID: 4769
    TicketEncryptionType: '0x17'
  condition: sel | count() by AccountName > 10
hardening: Use strong, long service-account passwords (or gMSA), disable RC4, and protect the krbtgt account.
enterprise: Kerberos abuse is central to Active Directory attack paths.

title: Sysmon & Event Logs
summary: System Monitor (Sysmon) enriches Windows logging with high-fidelity endpoint telemetry.
purpose: Sysmon records process, network, image-load and registry activity with hashes and full command lines - the raw material for detection.
architecture: A driver + service writing to the Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational log, driven by an XML config that filters noise.
dependencies: Best paired with a log forwarder (WEF) and a SIEM.
windows_logs: Security 4688/4624/4672; System 7045; the dedicated Sysmon/Operational channel.
linux_logs: Sysmon for Linux writes to syslog; auditd is the native equivalent.
detection: Alert on Security 1102 (log cleared) and on stops of the EventLog or Sysmon service.
sigma:
title: Windows Event Log Cleared
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  sel:
    EventID: 1102
  condition: sel
hardening: Deploy a tuned Sysmon config, forward logs off-host, and alert on logging tampering.
enterprise: A good Sysmon baseline is the single biggest boost to endpoint detection coverage.
